evidence_quotes.quote: Nintendo reserves the right to exclude consoles from its online service, which is her right.
Nowadays, it is very often the case that only licences can be withdrawn by the manufacturer at any time (mostly unilaterally and without compensation).
unless it necessarily requires an abo from the operator

paragraphs.text: I would ask you to question the practice of many manufacturers where registration/activation is mandatory for the use of the devices. Last but not least, this happened with a camera (Osmo Action) of DJI, which can only be used five times, despite some 300 purchase costs. At the same time, technical resources, shared workshops, advisory knowledge or professionals in the sense of a ‘sharing model’ should also be provided. The app will then also be allowed to access the site. Another example is Nintendo with Nintendo Switch 2. Nintendo reserves the right to exclude consoles from its online service, which is her right. However, the user account can no longer be created and games that have been purchased (including physical media) cannot be used. Thus, in this case, the console is useless if it is sold for use -> a factory reset removes the account and a new one cannot be created. I would also ask you to look at the purchase of software online. Nowadays, it is very often the case that only licences can be withdrawn by the manufacturer at any time (mostly unilaterally and without compensation). In my view, a paid product (unless it necessarily requires an abo from the operator) should also become the property of the buyer. Examples include video games (see Ubisoft’s Crew). Online services may be switched off (the manufacturer should not be obliged to continue to pay for products), but then use should be allowed in the single player or with a local network connection. I hope you can stop these and other practices. Nowadays, it is very often the case that only licences can be withdrawn by the manufacturer at any time (mostly unilaterally and without compensation).
